name: Nintendo DS
funFacts: Second best-selling game console of all time
First Nintendo handheld with dual screens
The touchscreen was operated with a stylus stored in the back
Had a built-in PictoChat app for local wireless messaging
history: The Nintendo DS was a revolutionary handheld that introduced dual screens and a touchscreen to mainstream gaming. Its unique design and library of games made it one of the best-selling consoles ever made.
improvements: Introduced dual screens, touchscreen, built-in Wi-Fi and microphone versus the Game Boy Advance SP

name: Nintendo DS Lite
funFacts: 40% smaller and 33% lighter than the original DS
Brighter screens with four brightness levels
Retained the GBA cartridge slot unlike later models
One of the most recognisable handheld designs ever made
history: The DS Lite was a slim redesign of the original DS that addressed nearly every criticism of the original. Its sleek look and improved screens made it the definitive version of the original DS hardware.
improvements: Slimmer design, brighter screens, longer battery life versus the original DS

name: Nintendo DSi
funFacts: First DS model with cameras — front and rear 0.3MP
Introduced the DSi Shop for downloadable DSiWare games
Removed the GBA cartridge slot to slim down the design
Had an SD card slot for storing photos and downloaded software
history: The DSi was a significant step forward for the DS family, adding cameras, an online shop and downloadable software for the first time. It was the first DS model to have its own exclusive software via DSiWare.
improvements: Added cameras, DSi Shop, SD card slot, larger screens versus the DS Lite

name: Nintendo DSi XL
funFacts: Screens were 93% larger than the original DS
Came with two styluses — a standard and a pen-style
Shipped with Brain Age Express pre-installed
Targeted at older audiences and families
history: The DSi XL took the DSi formula and scaled it up significantly. The large screens made it ideal for older players or those who found smaller handhelds uncomfortable, and it came with software pre-installed.
improvements: Much larger screens, longer battery life, pen stylus included versus the DSi

name: Nintendo 3DS
funFacts: First mainstream handheld with glasses-free 3D display
Featured a built-in pedometer that earned in-game coins
Included a gyroscope and motion sensor
Had a dedicated 3D depth slider to control the effect
history: The Nintendo 3DS was Nintendo's follow-up to the massively successful DS family. It introduced glasses-free stereoscopic 3D gaming to the mainstream, along with augmented reality features and StreetPass connectivity.
improvements: Added 3D display, gyroscope, motion controls, AR camera and StreetPass versus the DSi XL

name: Nintendo 3DS XL
funFacts: Screens were 90% larger than the original 3DS
Did not include an AC adapter in some regions to reduce cost
Became the best-selling model in the 3DS family
Dropped the external camera found on the original
history: The 3DS XL addressed the most common complaint about the original — screen size. The larger form factor made it more comfortable for extended play sessions and became the go-to model for many players.
improvements: Larger screens, improved battery life, more ergonomic design versus the original 3DS

name: Nintendo 2DS
funFacts: Launched the same day as Pokémon X and Y
Flat slate design — cannot fold like other models
Targeted at younger children due to lower price and no 3D
Despite the name, plays all 3DS games
history: The 2DS was Nintendo's budget entry point into the 3DS ecosystem, removing the 3D screen and hinge to cut costs. It was aimed at younger audiences and families who wanted 3DS game compatibility at a lower price.
improvements: Lower price point, no hinge to break, targeted at younger children versus the 3DS

name: New Nintendo 3DS
launchGame: The Legend of Zelda: Majora's Mask 3D
funFacts: Introduced a C-Stick nub for camera control
Added ZL and ZR shoulder buttons
Featured face-tracking to improve the 3D effect
Had exclusive games that only ran on the New 3DS
history: The New 3DS was a significant hardware revision with a faster CPU, more RAM, improved 3D head tracking and new controls. It introduced a new tier of exclusive software that the original 3DS could not run.
improvements: Faster CPU, more RAM, C-Stick, ZL/ZR buttons, face-tracking 3D versus the original 3DS

name: New Nintendo 3DS XL
funFacts: Xenoblade Chronicles 3D was a New 3DS exclusive at launch
Most popular model in many regions
Supported Amiibo via built-in NFC reader
MicroSD slot replaced the standard SD slot
history: Combining the larger screen of the XL with the improved internals of the New 3DS, the New 3DS XL became the definitive version of the hardware for most players and remained on sale until the end of the 3DS lifecycle.
improvements: Larger screens plus all New 3DS upgrades versus the original 3DS XL

name: New Nintendo 2DS XL
funFacts: Last model ever released in the DS/3DS family
Brought the clamshell design back to the 2DS line
Had New 3DS internals at a budget price
Released just months after the Nintendo Switch launched
history: The New 2DS XL was the final hardware revision in the DS/3DS family. It combined upgraded New 3DS internals with a folding clamshell design and large screens at a lower price than the New 3DS XL.
improvements: Clamshell design, New 3DS internals at lower cost versus the original 2DS
